text.title: Terraform State Pre-Deployment Checklist
text.summary: Backend setup, security, import process, migration, disaster recovery, and CI/CD integration checklists.

rule.statement: Create state infrastructure first (S3 bucket with versioning + KMS, DynamoDB table with prevent_destroy, IAM policy); then configure backend block; then run terraform init -migrate-state if migrating from local.
rule.rationale: Infrastructure must exist before backend initialization. Migration prompt during init copies local state to the remote backend atomically.

rule.statement: Separate state files by environment (dev/staging/prod) AND by component (networking/compute/database); use the key pattern {env}/{component}/terraform.tfstate.
rule.rationale: Single-state monorepo configurations have large blast radius — one mistake can destroy all production infrastructure. Component separation limits each apply's scope.

rule.statement: Enable encryption at rest (SSE-KMS), block all public access, enable access logging, and restrict bucket policy to Terraform CI/CD roles only.
rule.rationale: State files contain passwords and private keys. Encryption protects data at rest; access restrictions limit who can read those secrets.

rule.statement: Mark all sensitive outputs (passwords, connection strings, private keys) with sensitive = true; audit state for accidentally committed plaintext secrets quarterly.
rule.rationale: sensitive = true suppresses output in plan/apply logs. Regular audits catch cases where sensitive data was inadvertently written to state.

rule.statement: Write import blocks and matching resource configuration first; run terraform plan to preview the import; run terraform apply; then run terraform plan again to verify no drift.
rule.rationale: A clean second plan (no changes) confirms the imported resource configuration exactly matches actual cloud state. Any diff indicates missing arguments.

rule.statement: Enable versioning on state buckets; set lifecycle policy to expire non-current versions after 90 days; document state file locations for all environments; test recovery quarterly.
rule.rationale: Versioning is the only rollback mechanism for corrupted state. Untested recovery procedures fail under incident pressure.

rule.statement: Use OIDC authentication for CI/CD cloud provider access instead of static IAM access keys; set TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true in pipeline environments; implement plan/apply separation with human review.
rule.rationale: OIDC eliminates long-lived static credentials that can be leaked. TF_IN_AUTOMATION suppresses interactive prompts. Plan/apply separation requires human approval before production changes.

antipattern.description: Storing backend credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY) as static CI secrets.
antipattern.reason: Static keys never expire and are frequently leaked in CI logs. OIDC short-lived tokens are scoped to the specific workflow run and expire immediately after.
